title: Stack

sections.title: Introduction
concepts: Data structures — ways to organize and store data
Linear data structures — elements arranged in a sequential order
Non-linear data structures — elements not in sequence
Examples of linear data structures: arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues
Examples of non-linear data structures: trees, graphs
Importance of choosing the right data structure for efficiency

definitions.definition: A data structure is a particular way of organizing and storing data in a computer so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. It defines the relationship between the data and the operations that can be performed on it.

A linear data structure is one in which data elements are arranged in a sequential order, where each element has a predecessor and a successor (except the first and last elements). Examples: arrays, stacks, queues, linked lists.

key_points: Data structures help in organizing data for efficient access and modification
Linear data structures have elements in a sequential arrangement
Stacks and queues are special types of linear data structures with restricted operations
The choice of data structure depends on the nature of operations to be performed

sections.title: Stack
concepts: Stack follows LIFO (Last In First Out) principle
Elements are added and removed from the same end called the TOP
Real-world analogies: stack of plates, stack of books, stack of bangles
Applications of stack: string/expression reversal
Applications of stack: undo/redo operations in editors
Applications of stack: browser back button (history)
Applications of stack: parenthesis/bracket matching
Applications of stack: function call management (call stack)

definitions.definition: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the LIFO (Last In First Out) principle. Elements can only be added (pushed) or removed (popped) from one end called the TOP of the stack.

LIFO stands for Last In First Out. It means the last element added to the stack is the first element to be removed. The most recently pushed element is always at the top and is the first to be popped.
TOP is a pointer/variable that keeps track of the topmost element of the stack. It indicates the position where the next element will be added or from where the next element will be removed.
Stack overflow occurs when an attempt is made to push an element onto a stack that is already full (has reached its maximum capacity).
Stack underflow occurs when an attempt is made to pop an element from an empty stack (no elements to remove).

key_points: Stack follows LIFO — the last element inserted is the first to be removed
All insertions and deletions happen at the TOP of the stack
Stack has two main operations: PUSH (insert) and POP (delete)
Stack overflow occurs when pushing to a full stack
Stack underflow occurs when popping from an empty stack
Applications: expression evaluation, string reversal, undo operations, browser back button, parenthesis matching, function call management

sections.title: Operations on Stack
concepts: PUSH operation — adds an element to the top of the stack
POP operation — removes and returns the top element from the stack
PEEK/TOP operation — returns the top element without removing it
isEmpty — checks if the stack is empty
size — returns the number of elements in the stack
display — shows all elements in the stack

definitions.definition: PUSH is the operation of adding (inserting) a new element to the top of the stack. After a push operation, the new element becomes the topmost element and TOP is incremented.
POP is the operation of removing (deleting) the topmost element from the stack. The element at TOP is removed and returned, and TOP is decremented. POP on an empty stack causes underflow.

formulas.name: PUSH algorithm
formulas.formula:
Step 1: If stack is full, print 'Overflow' and return
Step 2: Increment TOP by 1
Step 3: Set stack[TOP] = element
Step 4: Return
formulas.notes: In Python using list, we use append() which handles resizing automatically

formulas.name: POP algorithm
formulas.formula:
Step 1: If stack is empty, print 'Underflow' and return
Step 2: Set element = stack[TOP]
Step 3: Decrement TOP by 1
Step 4: Return element
formulas.notes: In Python using list, we use pop() which removes and returns the last element

key_points: PUSH adds an element to the top; POP removes the top element
Before PUSH, check for overflow (stack full)
Before POP, check for underflow (stack empty)
PEEK returns the top element without removing it
In Python, lists can be used to implement stacks — append() for PUSH, pop() for POP

sections.title: Implementation of Stack in Python
concepts: Using Python list to implement a stack
append() method for PUSH operation
pop() method for POP operation
len() function to get stack size
Checking empty stack using len() == 0 or not operator
Accessing top element using index -1
Implementing stack operations as functions
Menu-driven program for stack operations

formulas.name: Stack implementation using list
formulas.formula:
stack = []  # empty stack
stack.append(element)  # PUSH
element = stack.pop()  # POP
top = stack[-1]  # PEEK
len(stack)  # SIZE
len(stack) == 0  # isEmpty
formulas.notes: Python lists grow dynamically, so no overflow check needed for list-based stacks

key_points: Python list is the simplest way to implement a stack
append() adds to the end (top) of the list — acts as PUSH
pop() removes and returns the last element — acts as POP
stack[-1] gives the top element without removing it — acts as PEEK
len(stack) == 0 checks if the stack is empty
Since Python lists are dynamic, overflow is generally not an issue
Always check for underflow (empty stack) before pop()

sections.title: Notations for Arithmetic Expressions
concepts: Infix notation — operator between operands (A + B)
Prefix notation (Polish notation) — operator before operands (+ A B)
Postfix notation (Reverse Polish notation) — operator after operands (A B +)
Infix is the most common human-readable form
Prefix and postfix do not require parentheses
Operator precedence: ** > *, /, % > +, -
Operator associativity: left to right (except ** which is right to left)
Computers evaluate postfix expressions more efficiently than infix

definitions.definition: In infix notation, the operator is placed between the two operands. Example: A + B, (A + B) * C. This is the most commonly used notation by humans but requires parentheses to override operator precedence.
In prefix notation, the operator is placed before its operands. Example: + A B, * + A B C. Named after Polish mathematician Jan Lukasiewicz. Does not require parentheses.

In postfix notation, the operator is placed after its operands. Example: A B +, A B + C *. Also called Reverse Polish Notation (RPN). Does not require parentheses and is easy for computers to evaluate using a stack.

formulas.name: Operator precedence (highest to lowest)
formulas.notes: ** is right-associative; all others are left-associative

formulas.name: Infix to Postfix conversion examples
formulas.formula:
A + B -> A B +
A + B * C -> A B C * +
(A + B) * C -> A B + C *
A + B * C - D -> A B C * + D -
(A + B) * (C - D) -> A B + C D - *
formulas.notes: Follow operator precedence and associativity rules during conversion

key_points: Infix: operator between operands — A + B
Prefix: operator before operands — + A B
Postfix: operator after operands — A B +
Postfix and prefix notations do not require parentheses
Computers use postfix form for efficient expression evaluation
Operator precedence determines the order of evaluation when no parentheses are present
Associativity determines order when operators have the same precedence

sections.title: Conversion from Infix to Postfix Notation
concepts: Algorithm uses a stack to hold operators and parentheses
Operands go directly to the output
Operators are pushed/popped based on precedence
Left parenthesis is pushed onto the stack
Right parenthesis causes popping until left parenthesis is found
At the end, remaining operators are popped from the stack

formulas.name: Infix to Postfix Algorithm
formulas.formula:
1. Create an empty stack and output list
2. Scan the infix expression left to right:
   a. If operand, add to output
   b. If '(', push onto stack
   c. If ')', pop and add to output until '(' is found; discard '('
   d. If operator, pop and add to output while stack top has >= precedence (for left-assoc) or > precedence (for right-assoc); then push current operator
3. Pop all remaining operators from stack to output
formulas.notes: Handle operator associativity: left-to-right for +, -, *, /; right-to-left for **

key_points: The algorithm uses a stack to temporarily hold operators
Operands are added directly to the output string
An operator is pushed onto the stack after popping operators of higher or equal precedence
Opening parenthesis is always pushed; closing parenthesis triggers popping until matching opening parenthesis
After scanning all tokens, pop remaining operators from the stack

sections.title: Evaluation of Postfix Expression
concepts: Postfix evaluation uses a stack for operands
Scan left to right — push operands, pop and evaluate on operators
When an operator is encountered, pop two operands, apply operator, push result
The final value in the stack is the result
Order matters for non-commutative operators (-, /, **)

formulas.name: Postfix Evaluation Algorithm
formulas.formula:
1. Create an empty stack
2. Scan the postfix expression left to right:
   a. If operand, push onto stack
   b. If operator, pop two operands (op2 = pop, op1 = pop), compute op1 operator op2, push result
3. The final element in the stack is the result
formulas.notes: op1 is the second popped element, op2 is the first popped. Order: op1 operator op2.

key_points: Scan the postfix expression from left to right
Push operands onto the stack
When an operator is found, pop two operands, apply the operator, and push the result
The second popped element is the left operand (op1), and the first popped is the right operand (op2)
For subtraction and division, the order matters: op1 - op2, op1 / op2
The last value remaining in the stack is the final result

summary: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the LIFO (Last In First Out) principle.
Elements can only be inserted (PUSH) or removed (POP) from the TOP of the stack.
Stack overflow occurs when pushing to a full stack; underflow occurs when popping from an empty stack.
In Python, a stack can be implemented using a list: append() for PUSH and pop() for POP.
Applications of stacks include: expression evaluation, string reversal, undo/redo operations, browser history, parenthesis matching, and function call management.
There are three notations for arithmetic expressions: Infix (A + B), Prefix (+ A B), and Postfix (A B +).
Postfix and prefix notations do not need parentheses and are easier for computers to evaluate.
Infix to postfix conversion uses a stack to handle operator precedence and associativity.
Postfix expression evaluation uses a stack: push operands, and when an operator is found, pop two operands, apply the operator, and push the result.

questions.question:
State True or False:
(a) Stack is a LIFO data structure.
(b) In a stack, elements can be added or removed from both ends.
(c) PUSH operation adds an element to the top of the stack.
(d) POP operation on an empty stack causes overflow.
(e) Postfix expressions require parentheses for correct evaluation.
questions.answer:
(a) True — Stack follows Last In First Out principle.
(b) False — Elements can only be added or removed from one end (the TOP).
(c) True — PUSH inserts an element at the top of the stack.
(d) False — POP on an empty stack causes underflow, not overflow. Overflow occurs when pushing to a full stack.
(e) False — Postfix expressions do not require parentheses. The order of operands and operators uniquely determines the evaluation.

questions.question:
Convert the following infix expressions to postfix:
(a) A + B - C
(b) (A + B) * (C - D)
(c) A * B + C / D
(d) ((A + B) * C - D) / E
(e) A + B * C ** D - E
questions.answer:
(a) A + B - C
Postfix: A B + C -

(b) (A + B) * (C - D)
Postfix: A B + C D - *

(c) A * B + C / D
Postfix: A B * C D / +

(d) ((A + B) * C - D) / E
Postfix: A B + C * D - E /

(e) A + B * C ** D - E
Postfix: A B C D ** * + E -
(Note: ** has highest precedence and is right-associative)

questions.question: Evaluate the following postfix expression: 5 3 + 8 2 - *
questions.answer:
Step-by-step evaluation:
Token | Stack        | Action
5     | [5]          | Push 5
3     | [5, 3]       | Push 3
+     | [8]          | Pop 3, 5; compute 5+3=8; Push 8
8     | [8, 8]       | Push 8
2     | [8, 8, 2]    | Pop 2, 8; (not yet — push 2 first)
-     | [8, 6]       | Pop 2, 8; compute 8-2=6; Push 6
*     | [48]         | Pop 6, 8; compute 8*6=48; Push 48

Result = 48

questions.question:
Write a Python program to implement a stack using a list with the following functions:
(a) push(stack, element) — to add an element
(b) pop(stack) — to remove and return the top element
(c) peek(stack) — to return the top element without removing
(d) display(stack) — to display all elements
(e) isEmpty(stack) — to check if the stack is empty
Write a Python program to check whether a given string is a palindrome using a stack.

What will be the output of the following code?
stack = []
stack.append(5)
stack.append(10)
stack.append(15)
print(stack.pop())
stack.append(20)
print(stack.pop())
print(stack.pop())
print(stack)
questions.answer:
Output:
15
20
10
[5]

Explanation:
- append(5) -> stack = [5]
- append(10) -> stack = [5, 10]
- append(15) -> stack = [5, 10, 15]
- pop() returns 15 -> stack = [5, 10] -> prints 15
- append(20) -> stack = [5, 10, 20]
- pop() returns 20 -> stack = [5, 10] -> prints 20
- pop() returns 10 -> stack = [5] -> prints 10
- print(stack) -> prints [5]

questions.question: Write a Python program to implement a stack of student records. Each record contains roll number and name. Implement push, pop, and display operations using a menu-driven approach.
